design_philosophy.core_loop: Collect objects (animals, food, Moses, sheep) and transport them to safe locations while avoiding enemies and hazards
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing collection/escort mechanics with evasion; player cannot harm most enemies, forcing avoidance over combat
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Participate in biblically-inspired stories (Noah, Moses, David) through interactive gameplay rather than passive narrative
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Religious educational content integrated directly into gameplay—Bible verses appear mid-level to restore health, making spiritual learning a mechanical necessity rather than optional flavor. Deliberately eschews violence (enemies cannot be harmed in most segments) in favor of non-lethal puzzle-solving, reflecting faith-based design philosophy

difficulty.lives_system: Health bar system replenished by collecting Bible verses scattered through levels
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based progression; no mid-level checkpoints documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited; difficulty appears fixed with no documented difficulty settings. Resource dependency (Bible verses for health) creates soft resource-management layer

progression.upgrade_system: Equipment acquisition within episodes (sling in David and Goliath segment; acorns for stunning beasts)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Three separate episodes available from start: Noah's Ark (4 levels), Baby Moses, David and Goliath; progression is sequential within each episode
replayability_hooks: Three distinct episodes with different mechanics reduce sameness on first pass
Resource collection challenge (Bible verses, animals, food) encourages optimization
High critical reception of 'poor design' and 'level recycling' may actually limit replayability appeal

world_building_techniques: Biblical source material (Old Testament stories: Noah's flood, Moses' escape, David vs Goliath)
Environmental hazards reflect narrative context (Nile in Moses level, lions/bears in David segment)
Collectible Bible verses serve dual purpose: mechanical health resource and narrative/thematic reinforcement
Loose adaptation of source material allows gameplay to drive narrative rather than vice versa

boss_design.philosophy: Climactic encounters tied to narrative arc; Goliath represents capstone challenge to David's progression from shepherd to warrior
notable_bosses: Goliath (David and Goliath finale) - single head-strike defeat condition, preceded by shield bearer encounter

faction_indicators: Christian/faith-based positioning: sold exclusively in Christian bookstores, not mainstream retail
Wisdom Tree as publisher signals faith-based developer community
Educational framing around Biblical literacy and religious participation
danger_signals: Critical reception heavily criticized 'didactic' approach—gameplay interrupted by Bible verses felt intrusive to core gameplay loop
Overly literal adherence to non-violence philosophy may frustrate action-game expectations
Religious content gatekeeping (bookstore-only distribution) limited mainstream cultural penetration
environmental_storytelling: Ark structure in Noah segment signals collection-goal narrative
Nile River prominence in Moses level reinforces historical/religious context
Cave system with hidden snakes adds secret-discovery layer to environmental design
Pastoral sheep-herding opening in David segment establishes humble origins before warrior transformation
